MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Apr-29-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. WOODY WU, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of SAC Attorneys (tel: 408-837-9133) for Plaintiff.
Defendant QINGLING XU, in pro per present.
Defendant YANYING LIU, in pro per present (via zoom videoconference).

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF TIEMING YAO's Motion For Summary Judgment.

Matter argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Tieming Yao's motion for summary judgment is denied. A summary judgment motion "shall be heard no later than 30 days before the date of trial, unless the court for good cause orders otherwise." (CCP 437c(a)(3).) Trial in this matter is set for May 5, 2025. Yao offers no basis to support a good cause finding.

Independently, even were the court to reach the merits, the court concludes that a triable issue of fact exists on whether defendant Yanying Liu transferred his interests in the sale of the 745 Victoria Street property "[w]ith actual intent to hinder, delay, or defraud any creditor of the debtor." (CCP 3439.04(a)(1).) "Whether a debtor had the actual intent to hinder, delay, or defraud a creditor is a question of fact." (Aghaian v. Minassian (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 447, 456.) While plaintiff Yao points to the presence of a number of indicia of bad intent, Liu and co-defendant Qingling Xu present evidence that the transfer was instead motivated by a desire to keep the family assets in the United States where the couple's children resided when the marriage resolved. (Defendants' Declaration on the Status of Two Defendants' '745 Property,' pp. 4-8.) Because there is a triable issue of fact on the second cause of action for violation of the uniform voidable transactions act, and Yao has moved only for summary judgment on the entire case and not summary adjudication on individual causes of action, the court need not consider whether there is a triable issue of fact on the first cause of action for recognition of the foreign judgment.

Order submitted.

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-05-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. WOODY WU, Esq. of SAC Attorneys, LLP (tel: 408-436-0789) for Plaintiff.
Atty. LAURA CAMPBELL, Esq. of Nixon Peabody LLP (tel:415-984-8200) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT YANYING LIU, QINGLING XU's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Yanying Liu and Qingling Xu's demurrer to plaintiff Tieming Yao's third amended complaint is overruled.

Yao began a breach of contract action in this court that was stayed in 2018 pending the dispute's resolution in China. In 2021, this court lifted the stay after the Chinese court entered judgment in Yao's favor. In 2023, Yao filed a third amended complaint ("TAC") for (1) recognition of foreign country judgment, and (2) violation of the Uniform Voidable Transactions Act ("UVTA"). Liu and Xu demur to the entire TAC.

First, defendants argue Yao failed to join indispensable parties. (CCP 430.10(d).) CCP 389 governs the joinder of parties to litigation, with subdivision (b) listing four factors to determine whether a party is indispensable. Defendants do not analyze these factors, instead citing Sierra Club, Inc. v. California Coastal Com. (1979) 95 Cal.App.3d 495, 501, for the proposition that prejudice to an absent party establishes that party is indispensable. That rule is outdated. (See Quantification Settlement Agreement Cases (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 758, 857.) Accordingly, defendants have not carried their burden in proving there are indispensable parties.

Second, defendants argue the cause of action for violation of the UVTA fails to state a claim. (CCP 430.10(e).) "A fraudulent transfer under the UVTA is a transfer by the debtor of property to a third person undertaken with the intent to prevent a creditor from reaching that interest to satisfy its claim. [Citations and quotations omitted.]" (Potter v. Alliance United Ins. Co. (2019) 37 Cal.App.5th 894, 903-904.) Here, defendants argue Xu never concealed the transfer and request judicial notice of their recorded deed. The request is granted as to the deed's existence only, not to facts stated therein such as payment of "valuable consideration." (See RFJN Ex. 1; Herrera v. Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 1366, 1375.) Even so, Yao alleges Xu transferred $900k to his wife after he had been sued, absconded, and now claims insolvency. (TAC para. 24-31.) These allegations suffice at the pleadings stage.

Third, defendants argue the TAC fails for uncertainty because Yao mentions someone named You but does not clarify You's relationship to the underlying causes of action. (CCP 430.010(f).) "Demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond." (Morris v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2022) 78 Cal.App.5th 279, 292.) These references to You do not render the TAC incomprehensible or uncertain. Defendants can reasonably respond to allegations of a fraudulent transfer between themselves and recognition of a foreign judgment against them.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RCE)

Notice Of Motion And Renewed Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action For Forum Non Conveniens
Off Calendar Feb-28-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS YANYING LIU, QINGLING XU'S Renewed Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action For Forum Non Conveniens is denied.

"In determining whether to grant a motion based on forum non conveniens, a court must first determine whether the alternate forum is a 'suitable' place for trial." (Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal. 3d 744, 751.) "The defendant, as the moving party, bears the burden of proof on a motion based on forum non conveniens." (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal. App. 4th 902, 918.) "An alternative forum is suitable if it has jurisdiction and the action in that forum will not be barred by the statute of limitations." (Guimei v. Gen. Elec. Co. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 689, 696; see also Zhi An Wang v. Fang (2021) 59 Cal.App.5th 907, 918.) "[A] forum is suitable where an action can be brought, although not necessarily won." (Zhi An Wang, 59 Cal.App.5th at 918 (internal quotations omitted).) Defendants indicate they are willing to submit to jurisdiction in North Carolina. (See Stangvik, 54 Cal.3d at 752.) Defendants also introduce evidence that Plaintiff filed a complaint against them in North Carolina, thereby conceding that Defendants are subject to jurisdiction there. (Def.'s Jan. 26, 2022 RJN.) Defendants also establish that there is no statute of limitations issue. Defendants therefore meet their burden to show that North Carolina is a suitable forum.

However, "the private interests of the litigants and the interests of the public in retaining the action for trial in California" outweigh the interests in litigating in North Carolina. (Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal. 3d 744, 751.) The only remaining issue to be resolved in this matter is whether the money judgment Plaintiff obtained against Defendant Liu in the People's Court of Yunyan District, Guiyang City, China is entitled to recognition under California's Uniform Foreign-Country Money Judgments Recognition Act. (Cal. Civ. Proc. Code sec. 1713 et seq.) Other than the existence of the money judgment itself, Plaintiff contends that no proof is required and no witnesses need be called to determine whether the Chinese judgment is entitled to recognition in California. Court congestion is not a factor where there is a single Plaintiff. (See Roulier v. Cannondale (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1180, 1190.) The interests of potential jurors are irrelevant because the Court, not a jury, will decide whether the Chinese judgment should be recognized. Furthermore, the court in North Carolina found that "the dispute which gives rise to this lawsuit is subject to California law, and the California Lawsuit was filed before this action. It would work substantial injustice for the action to be tried in a court of this State and the parties are entitled to have this matter stayed." (Pl.'s RJN, Ex. B, 2-3.) Defendants owned a home in San Francisco from 2012 to 2018. Accordingly, they were California residents in 2015 when Defendant Liu allegedly breached his obligation to repay Plaintiff. Plaintiff's choice to file in California is also entitled to deference. (See Nat'l Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 929; Stangvik, 54 Cal.3d at 753.) The Court therefore finds that the private and public interest weigh in favor of retaining the action in California.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

MOTION TO DISMISS
Off Calendar Oct-13-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS YANYING LIU and QINGLING XU's MOTION TO DISMISS:

Defendants Yanying Liu et al.'s motion to dismiss for forum non conveniens is denied. "In determining whether to grant a motion based on forum non conveniens, a court must first determine whether the alternate forum is a 'suitable' place for trial." (Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal. 3d 744, 751.) "The defendant, as the moving party, bears the burden of proof on a motion based on forum non conveniens." (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal. App. 4th 902, 918.)

The only remaining issue to be resolved in this matter is whether the money judgment Plaintiff Tieming Yao obtained against Defendant Liu in the People's Court of Yunyan District, Guiyang City, China is entitled to recognition under California's Uniform Foreign-Country Money Judgments Recognition Act. (Code Civ. Proc. sections 1713-1752.) By definition, California is the only suitable forum in which to determine whether the Chinese judgment should be recognized in this State. Defendants offer no evidence or coherent reason as to why a Chinese court or a court in North Carolina would be a more suitable forum to resolve this issue. They instead argue repetitively that the Court is compelled to dismiss the action because the parties reside outside of California and have no assets here. A court may only consider these factors, however, once it has determined that an alternate forum is suitable. (Invs. Equity Life Holding Co. v. Schmidt (2015) 233 Cal. App. 4th 1363, 1368 ["It is only after the trial court reaches that conclusion that it would even consider whether the benefits of the proposed alternative forum outweigh the reasons for keeping the litigation in California."].) China and North Carolina are not suitable forums to litigate the remaining issue in this matter. As such, the Court need not consider "the private interests of the litigants and the interests of the public in retaining the action for trial in California." (Stangvik, 54 Cal. 3d at 751.)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312 via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Supplemental Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motion
Off Calendar Aug-12-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF TIEMING YAO'S Motion For Leave To File Supplemental Complaint:

Plaintiff Tieming Yao's motion for leave to file supplemental complaint is granted. A supplemental complaint is appropriate to allege relevant facts occurring after the original pleading was filed-here, the entry of the Chinese judgment. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 464.) The proposed supplemental complaint does not allege a wholly "new" cause of action or defense, but merely supplements the originally pleaded claims by seeking recovery from defendant of a portion of the same funds allegedly owed on the same contract. (See, e.g., Honig v. Financial Corp. of America (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 960, 965-967 [holding that trial court abused its discretion in refusing to permit amendment to allege facts which occurred after the initial complaint, which related to the same general set of facts].) Nor is it precluded by Code of Civil Procedure section 1718, which explicitly recognizes that recognition of a foreign-country judgment may be sought "in a pending action." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1718(b); see also Hyundai Securities Co., Ltd. v. Lee (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-694 [holding only that a foreign-country money judgment must be recognized upon a duly noticed motion for summary judgment, judgment on the pleadings, or trial, rather than upon a petition].) From the standpoint of judicial economy and avoiding claim splitting, this approach is far superior to requiring plaintiff to file an entirely new action. Defendants do not show that they will be prejudiced in any way. If they wish to challenge the adequacy of the proposed supplemental complaint, they may do by way of an appropriate motion. (See California Casualty Gen. Ins. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 281 ["the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings."].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Plaintiff Tieming Yao Motion For Summary Judgment Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 Of 2)
Off Calendar May-28-2021 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar May-28-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Atty. WOODY WU, Esq. of SAC Attorneys, LLP (Tel: 408-436-0789) for plaintiff;
Atty. RENO FERNANDEZ, Esq. of MacDonald Fernandez LLP (Tel: 415-362-0449) for defendants.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

Matter argued and submited. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for summary judgment is denied. "[A] plaintiff, in order to obtain recognition of a foreign-country money judgment must proceed in accordance with the normal procedures applicable to actions and not by way of a motion or petition...." (Hyundai Sec. Co. v. Lee (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 682, 693; see also Manco Contracting Co. (W.L.L.) v. Bezdikian (2008) 45 Cal.4th 192, 207 ["California has not enacted an expedited procedure for the registration of foreign judgments. Rather, a party seeking recognition of a foreign judgment... must file a civil action."].)

Plaintiff's operative complaint is the First Amended Complaint filed on June 27, 2018. That pleading makes no mention of a foreign judgment-obviously, because it did not exist at that time.

Because the operative complaint does not mention the foreign judgment, this motion is procedurally defective. In other words, a plaintiff cannot obtain summary judgment on a matter not pleaded in his/her complaint.

"The purpose of a summary judgment proceeding is to permit a party to show that material factual claims arising from the pleadings need not be tried because they are not in dispute. The function of the pleadings in a motion for summary judgment is to delimit the scope of the issues: the function of the affidavits or declarations is to disclose whether there is any triable issue of fact within the issues delimited by the pleadings. The complaint measures the materiality of the facts tendered in a defendant's challenge to the plaintiff's cause of action." (FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 381 (internal quotations and citations omitted).)

The court concludes that plaintiff needs to file a new action for this court to recognize the judgment. First, the language of CCP 1718 supports this conclusion. (See Manco Contracting Co. (W.L.L.) v. Bezdikian (2008) 45 Cal.4th 192, 207, fn. 12.)

CCP sec. 1718 provides:

(a) If recognition of a foreign-country judgment is sought as an original matter, the issue of recognition shall be raised by filing an action seeking recognition of the foreign-country judgment.

(b) If recognition of a foreign-country judgment is sought in a pending action, the issue of recognition may be raised by counterclaim, cross-claim, or affirmative defense.

Second, the Chinese judgment arose after the filing of this action and plaintiff would need to file a supplemental complaint. The general rule is that a supplemental complaint cannot plead an entirely new cause of action, however. (See Flood v. Simpson (1975) 45 Cal.App.3d 644, 647 ["[T]he court properly denied the motion on the basis that the supplement to the complaint sought to introduce new causes of action."]; but see Honig v. Financial Corp. of America (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 960, 966 [permitting a defamation claim that arose after the filing of the underlying harassment action].)

Counsel for plaintiff to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR No. 5041, Email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Lift Stay And To Recognize Foreign Money Judgment
Off Calendar Jan-14-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via ZOOM Video-conferencing:

No appearance is made in this matter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to lift stay and recognize foreign money judgment is granted in part and denied in part. Plaintiff's motion to lift the stay is granted, as Plaintiff has shown that the conditions of the stay, that the Chinese court resolve the dispute between Mr. Yao and Mr. Lui, have been met. (Wang Decl. 1:27 - 2:2.) The Court's September 24, 2018 order granting Defendants' motion to dismiss or stay action provided that "[o]nce the Chinese court resolves the dispute between Mr. Yao and Mr. Lui, Mr. Yao can return to the court to lift the stay and seek any other appropriate remedies." Plaintiff brought this motion on the basis that the Chinese court resolved the dispute between Mr. Yao and Mr. Lui as is evidenced by the inclusion of the judgment from the court in Yunyan China in this motion. Further, Plaintiff's counsel in Beijing attested to a civil judgment. (See Wang Decl. 1:27 - 2:2.) Considering no opposition was presented against lifting the stay in this action, the Court finds good cause to lift the stay.

Plaintiff's motion to recognize foreign money judgment is denied as the Court cannot recognize a foreign judgment by motion or petition. (Hyundai Sec. Co. v. Lee (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 682, 689-691.) Plaintiff concedes on reply that a judgment may not be entered based on this motion. Accordingly, Plaintiff's motion to recognize foreign money judgment is denied without prejudice. If Plaintiff believes that the matter may be resolved on motion, it may file an appropriate noticed motion without leave of Court or any need to reserve a hearing date.

Written order is signed.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-18-2018 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT YANYING LIU, QINGLING XU's DEMURRER TO VERIFIED COMPLAINT.

Defendants Yanying Liu and Qingling Xu's demurrer to all causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Tieming Yao is sustained as to all causes of action with 15 days leave to amend. The allegations of the complaint and the attachments are not inconsistent. Mr. Yao alleges that the individuals had an agreement and the payments were merely made through the corporations. The attachments are consistent with this theory of liability and Mr. Yao's allegations are accepted as true on this demurrer. Nonetheless, the court's copy of the complaint is missing exhibits J and K, which Mr. Yao references. Paragraph 3 of exhibit K supports Mr. Yao's position that the individuals had an agreement. Mr. Yao has leave to correct the error in failing to attach exhibits J and K. Once those documents are attached, the court does not expect to see another demurrer because the attachments are consistent with Mr. Yao's theory of liability and extrinsic evidence is necessary to determine the parties to the loans.
